# Requirements and Fees for a Valid Large Generator Interconnection Application in ISO New England

Based on the **ISO-NE Tariff Schedule 22 – Large Generator Interconnection Procedures (LGIP)** (effective May 3, 2025, per FERC Docket ER25-2149-000), the **ISO-NE Interconnection Process Guide** (web guide), the **Appendix 1 Interconnection Request** form, the **ISO-NE Interconnection Technical Notice on Site Control** (June 2, 2025), and associated FERC Order No. 2023 compliance materials, the following is a comprehensive summary of what is required to submit a valid interconnection application for a Large Generating Facility (>20 MW) in ISO New England.

---

## 1. Pre-Application Prerequisites

Before submitting an interconnection request (IR), the Interconnection Customer must complete three registration steps:

| Requirement | Description | Timeline |
|---|---|---|
| **ISO Customer ID** | Obtain an ISO New England Customer ID by submitting a "Request Generation Interconnect Customer ID" form to memcoord@iso-ne.com. The customer name must match across all ISO registrations. | 5–10 business days |
| **CEII Access** | Request and obtain Interconnection Request (IR) Critical Energy Infrastructure Information (CEII) clearance through the ISO's Request CEII Access page. | 5–10 business days |
| **IRTT Account** | Register for an account in the Interconnection Request Tracking Tool (IRTT). Existing customers can register directly; new customers must first obtain a Customer ID. | ~10 business days |

> **Source:** ISO-NE Interconnection Process Guide – "Register for an IRTT Account" and "Submit an Interconnection Request During Cluster Request Window" pages ([iso-ne.com](https://www.iso-ne.com/participate/applications-status-changes/interconnection-process-guide/submit-an-interconnection-request)); ISO-NE Interconnection Process Training, February 16, 2023 ([PDF](https://www.iso-ne.com/static-assets/documents/2023/02/20230216-interconnection-process-presentation.pdf)).

---

## 2. Timing: Cluster Request Window

Pursuant to FERC Order No. 2023, ISO-NE has transitioned from a serial first-come, first-served process to a **first-ready, first-served cluster study process**. Interconnection requests can **only** be submitted during a **45-calendar-day Cluster Request Window**.

- The next Cluster Request Window is expected to be open from **October 5, 2026 to November 19, 2026**.
- All IRs submitted and validated within a single Cluster Request Window are considered **equally queued** (i.e., they receive the same queue position relative to each other).

> **Source:** ISO-NE Interconnection Process Guide ([iso-ne.com](https://www.iso-ne.com/participate/applications-status-changes/interconnection-process-guide)); Schedule 22, Section 4.1.1; ISO-NE Order No. 2023 Compliance Overview, September 27, 2023 ([PDF](https://www.iso-ne.com/static-assets/documents/100003/a04a_2023_09_27_tc_order2023_compliance_overview.pdf)).

---

## 3. Completed Interconnection Request Form (Appendix 1 to Schedule 22)

The IR must be submitted using the **Appendix 1 Interconnection Request** form, which requires the following information:

### A. Project Information
- **Proposed Project Name**
- **Purpose of the request** (check one):
  - A proposed new Large Generating Facility
  - An increase in generating capacity or a modification that has the potential to be a Material Modification of an existing Generating Facility
  - Commencement of participation in the wholesale markets by an existing Generating Facility
  - A change from Network Resource Interconnection Service to Capacity Network Resource Interconnection Service

### B. Type of Interconnection Service Requested (check one)
- **Network Resource (NR) Interconnection Service** — energy capability only; enables participation in all energy markets, except the capacity market.
- **Capacity Network Resource (CNR) Interconnection Service** — energy and capacity capability; enables participation in both energy and capacity markets.
- Option to request downgrade to NR Service if violations are identified in the thermal analysis associated with CNR testing.

### C. Technical/Project Details
- **Address or location of the facility** (including Town/City, County, and State)
- **Requested Point of Interconnection (POI)**
- **Type of Generating Facility** to be constructed
- **Electric storage capacity** information (if applicable): whether the facility includes electric storage, whether it charges from the Administered Transmission System, aggregate charging capability at the POI, reactive capability at the high side of the main transformer, and primary frequency response operating range
- **Generating Facility Fuel Type**
- **Generating Facility Capacity (MW)** at four temperature bands:
  - At or above 90°F, 50°F, 20°F, and 0°F
  - For each: Maximum Gross MW Electrical Output, Maximum Net MW Electrical Output, and Net MW Capability at the POI
- **Requested Interconnection Service levels (in MW)**: CNR Capability (Summer/Winter), NR Capability (Summer/Winter)
- **Equipment configuration description**, including number of units, GSUs, and any proposed control technologies to restrict output
- **Requested Commercial Operation Date**
- **Requested Initial Synchronization Date**
- **Requested In-Service Date**

### D. Customer Information
- Company Name, ISO Customer ID#, address, company representative name/title/phone/email
- Authorized signature, printed name, title, and date

> **Source:** Appendix 1 to Schedule 22 – Interconnection Request form ([PDF](https://www.iso-ne.com/static-assets/documents/100030/oatt-schedule-22-appendix-1-large-generator-lgip-interconnection-request-with-attachments.pdf)); ISO-NE Interconnection Process Guide – "Submit an Interconnection Request" page.

---

## 4. Financial Requirements: Deposits and Fees

A valid IR must be accompanied by **multiple financial commitments** at the time of submission during the Cluster Request Window. The following table summarizes all required deposits:

| Deposit/Fee | Amount | Refundable? | Applied Toward |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Initial Deposit (Application Fee)** | **$50,000** | Potentially non-refundable (becomes non-refundable 10 business days after the scoping meeting) | Costs incurred by the ISO associated with the IR, development of study agreements and their attachments, and development of the LGIA |
| **Study Deposit** (new NR or CNR request) | **$250,000** | Refundable (applied to study costs; unspent portions refunded) | Cost of the Cluster Study |
| **Study Deposit** (request to change from existing NR to CNR) | **$100,000** | Refundable | Cost of the Cluster Study |
| **Commercial Readiness Deposit (CRD)** | Per FERC Order No. 2023 requirements (ISO-NE adopted the Order's CRD framework; the standard amount under Order No. 2023 is based on generating facility size, with ISO-NE's implementation detailed in the CRD Instructions on the Order No. 2023 Key Project page) | May be provided as cash (electronic transfer) or irrevocable Letter of Credit from an approved financial institution, or a combination | Demonstrates commercial readiness to proceed; subject to forfeiture upon withdrawal |
| **Site Control Deposit** (in lieu of Site Control, NR only, if Regulatory Limitation claimed) | **$10,000/MW**, subject to a **minimum of $500,000** and a **maximum of $2,000,000** | Refundable within the cure period per Section 3.4.3 of the LGIP | Held until Site Control is demonstrated; must demonstrate full Site Control within 180 calendar days of executing an Interconnection Agreement |

### Additional Study-Stage Deposits (Later in the Process)

| Deposit | Amount | Trigger |
|---|---|---|
| **Cluster Restudy Deposit** (if required) | Additional deposit equal to **5% of the network upgrade cost** assigned from the Cluster Study | If a Cluster Restudy is needed |
| **Interconnection Facilities Study Deposit** | The **greater of 25% of the estimated cost of the Facilities Study or $250,000** | When entering the Facilities Study stage |
| **Actual Study Costs** | Interconnection Customer pays actual costs of all studies; invoiced monthly | Throughout the study process |

> **Sources:**
> - Schedule 22, Section 3.4.1.1 — initial deposit and study deposit amounts ([PDF](https://www.iso-ne.com/static-assets/documents/regulatory/tariff/sect_2/sch22/sch_22_lgip.pdf))
> - Appendix 1 to Schedule 22 — Site Control deposit ($10,000/MW, min $500K, max $2M) ([PDF](https://www.iso-ne.com/static-assets/documents/100030/oatt-schedule-22-appendix-1-large-generator-lgip-interconnection-request-with-attachments.pdf))
> - Appendix 3 to Schedule 22 — Facilities Study deposit (greater of 25% or $250,000) ([PDF](https://www.iso-ne.com/static-assets/documents/100030/oatt-schedule-22-appendix-3-large-generator-lgip-interconnection-facilities-study-agreement.pdf))
> - ISO-NE Order No. 2023 Compliance Overview, September 27, 2023 — Cluster Restudy deposit (5% of network upgrade cost) and CRD framework ([PDF](https://www.iso-ne.com/static-assets/documents/100003/a04a_2023_09_27_tc_order2023_compliance_overview.pdf))
> - ISO-NE Order No. 2023 Compliance Presentation, January 23, 2024 — Section 3.4.1.1 deposit language ([PDF](https://www.iso-ne.com/static-assets/documents/100007/a03.1b_2024_01_23_tc_order2023_compliance_presentation.pdf))
> - Eversource summary of ISO-NE deposits (as of April 1, 2023) ([eversource.com](https://www.eversource.com/business/about/doing-business-with-us/transmission-interconnections/transmission-interconnection-process))
> - ISO-NE Order No. 2023 Key Project page — CRD Instructions, FAQs, and approved templates ([iso-ne.com](https://iso-ne.com/committees/key-projects/order-no-2023-key-project))
> - ISO-NE Interconnection Technical Notice on Site Control, June 2, 2025 ([PDF](https://www.iso-ne.com/static-assets/documents/100024/notice_re_regulatory_limitations_and_site_control_6_2_25.pdf))

---

## 5. Site Control Requirements

Site Control is a critical validity requirement. The rules differ by service type:

### For CNR Interconnection Service (Capacity):
- **100% exclusive Site Control** in the Interconnection Customer's name is **required without exception**.

### For NR Interconnection Service (Energy):
- **100% exclusive Site Control** in the Interconnection Customer's name is required, **OR**
- In lieu of Site Control, if a **Regulatory Limitation** prevents obtaining Site Control prior to submission, the customer may submit:
  1. A **$10,000/MW cash deposit** (minimum $500,000; maximum $2,000,000), refundable within the cure period per Section 3.4.3;
  2. A **signed affidavit** from an officer of the company indicating that Site Control is unobtainable due to Regulatory Limitations; and
  3. **Documentation** sufficiently describing and explaining the source and effects of such Regulatory Limitations, including conditions that must be met and the anticipated time to satisfy them.

### For Modifications to Existing Facilities:
- Site Control documentation is not required if the proposed modification is to the customer's existing Large Generating Facility and does not require additional real property (customer certifies it has Site Control).

### Acceptable Forms of Site Control Documentation:
- (a) Fee simple ownership or easement;
- (b) Valid written leasehold or other contractual interest;
- (c) Valid written option to purchase or leasehold interest;
- (d) Duly executed written contract to purchase, acquire an easement, license, or leasehold; or
- (e) Filed applications for required permits to site on federal or state property.

### Timing:
- Site Control documents must be submitted **as part of the IR through IRTT during the Cluster Request Window**.
- If a Regulatory Limitation is accepted, the customer must demonstrate **full Site Control within 180 calendar days** of executing an Interconnection Agreement.

> **Source:** ISO-NE Interconnection Technical Notice on Site Control, June 2, 2025 ([PDF](https://www.iso-ne.com/static-assets/documents/100024/notice_re_regulatory_limitations_and_site_control_6_2_25.pdf)); Appendix 1 to Schedule 22; Schedule 22, Section 3.4.2.

---

## 6. Technical Data Requirements

In addition to the Appendix 1 form, the Interconnection Customer must submit **technical data** sufficient for the ISO to perform the Cluster Study. The ISO provides guidance through the **Interconnection Request Technical Data Submittal Guidance** document. Key technical data includes:

- **Site electrical one-line diagram** reflecting the Generating Facility's configuration
- **Generator/converter specifications** (e.g., manufacturer model sheets, P-Q capability curves, ride-through characteristics for inverter-based resources)
- **Short circuit and dynamic modeling data** (e.g., manufacturer-provided models in PSS/E, PSCAD, or equivalent formats)
- **Reactive power capability** data
- **Primary frequency response** parameters (especially for electric storage resources)
- **Point of Interconnection** details and any proposed interconnection facilities

The ISO references **Planning Procedure No. 5-6 (PP 5-6)** for the scope and assumptions of interconnection studies, and **Operating Procedure 14 (OP-14)** for technical requirements for generators.

> **Source:** ISO-NE Interconnection Process Guide – "Submit an Interconnection Request" page; ISO-NE Interconnection Process Training, February 16, 2023 ([PDF](https://www.iso-ne.com/static-assets/documents/2023/02/20230216-interconnection-process-presentation.pdf)).

---

## 7. Validation Process

Once the IR is submitted through IRTT during the Cluster Request Window:

1. **Within 5 business days** of receipt, the ISO will notify the Interconnection Customer whether the IR is **valid** or identify any **deficiencies** (including deficiencies related to Site Control, deposits, or technical data).
2. If deficiencies are identified, the customer has a **cure period** (generally 10 business days) to cure the deficiencies.
3. Once the IR is deemed valid, the ISO assigns a **Queue Position**. All valid IRs submitted within the same Cluster Request Window are considered **equally queued**.
4. Within **3 business days** after the close of the Cluster Request Window, the ISO submits copies of all valid IRs to the relevant Interconnecting Transmission Owner(s).

> **Source:** Schedule 22, Sections 3.4.4 and 4.1.1; ISO-NE Interconnection Technical Notice on Site Control; ISO-NE Order No. 2023 Compliance Presentation, January 23, 2024 ([PDF](https://www.iso-ne.com/static-assets/documents/100007/a03.1b_2024_01_23_tc_order2023_compliance_presentation.pdf)).

---

## 8. Summary Checklist for a Valid Large Generator Interconnection Request

| # | Requirement | Details |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | ISO Customer ID | Obtained prior to submission |
| 2 | CEII Access | IR CEII clearance obtained |
| 3 | IRTT Account | Active account in the Interconnection Request Tracking Tool |
| 4 | Submit during Cluster Request Window | 45-calendar-day window (next: Oct 5 – Nov 19, 2026) |
| 5 | Completed Appendix 1 Interconnection Request form | All project, technical, and customer information fields completed and signed |
| 6 | Initial Deposit | $50,000 (potentially non-refundable) |
| 7 | Study Deposit | $250,000 (new NR/CNR) or $100,000 (NR-to-CNR change) |
| 8 | Commercial Readiness Deposit (CRD) | Per Order No. 2023 requirements; cash or Letter of Credit |
| 9 | Site Control | 100% exclusive Site Control for CNR (mandatory); 100% for NR or $10,000/MW deposit (min $500K, max $2M) + affidavit + documentation if Regulatory Limitation claimed |
| 10 | Technical Data | Complete technical data package per PP 5-6 and the Interconnection Request Technical Data Submittal Guidance |
| 11 | Type of Service Election | NR (energy only) or CNR (energy + capacity) |
| 12 | Point of Interconnection | Definitive POI identified |

---

## Key Governing Documents

| Document | Description |
|---|---|
| **Schedule 22, LGIP** (effective 5/3/2025, ER25-2149-000) | Primary tariff governing large generator interconnection procedures |
| **Appendix 1 to Schedule 22** | Interconnection Request form |
| **Appendix 2 to Schedule 22** | Cluster Study Agreement

      "text": "Interconnection Process Guide\n\n/isoexpress/webservices/-/webservice/getSignInInfo\n\n/isoexpress/webservices/-/webservice/authenticate\n\n# Interconnection Process Guide\n\n### In this section\n\n- Interconnection Process Guide\n- Is the ISO New England Interconnection Process Right for You?\n- Register for an IRTT Account\n- Submit an Interconnection Request During Cluster Request Window\n- Register Your Asset\n\n### Related Links\n\n- Glossary of Interconnection-Related Acronyms\n- Interconnection-Related Governing Documents\n- Contact IRTT Support\n\nISO New England is providing this web guide to assist you in understanding the interconnection process for small and large generating facilities and elective transmission upgrades (ETUs) to interconnect to the administered transmission system. However, you shouldn’t rely solely on this web guide. Consult the ISO New England Inc. Transmission, Markets, and Services Tariff(ISO Tariff), Section II, known as the ISO’s Open Access Transmission Tariff(OATT), and relevant planning procedures and operating procedures to ensure that you are complying with all requirements. In case of any discrepancy with this web guide, the ISO Tariff and applicable operating procedures and planning procedures govern.\n\n## Overview of the Interconnection Process\n\nAs a Regional Transmission Organization (RTO), the ISO coordinates how transmission lines, power plants, and other resources connect to and operate on New England’s administered transmission system to ensure reliability and facilitate wholesale electricity market participation. The ISO does not administer the interconnection process for projects subject to state interconnection processes. Thus, after you determine that the ISO interconnection process applies to your project, you’ll need to submit an interconnection request to the ISO through the Interconnection Request Tracking Tool(IRTT). Existing customers can register for an account using IRTT, while new customers must first or simultaneously obtain an ISO customer ID and access to critical energy infrastructure information.\n\nAs required by the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission’s Order No. 2023, it is only possible to submit interconnection requests during a cluster request window. The next window to submit a complete interconnection request package for the next Cluster Study is expected to be open from October 5, 2026 to November 19, 2026. After the ISO validates your interconnection request and the cluster request window closes, the customer engagement window opens and a cluster study scoping meeting is held. A cluster study scoping meeting will address pertinent information about your project and the interconnection studies the ISO will perform to assess your project’s impact on the power system and the facilities and upgrades potentially needed to interconnect your project per all requirements and standards.\n\nAfter the ISO has completed the necessary study work, you’ll work with the ISO and the transmission owner on an Interconnection Agreement (IA), a formal document detailing the terms and conditions for interconnection service. You must enter into an IA before you can construct or operate a generator or an elective transmission upgrade. Final steps of the interconnection process are to submit final data and register your asset with the ISO.\n\n## Governing Documents\n\nSeveral schedules of the ISO’s Open Access Transmission Tariff contain the rules detailing the interconnection process:\n\nSection II, ISO New England Open Access Transmission Tariff(OATT)\n\n- Helps ensure that all transmission owners and transmission customers have open access to transmission service in New England\n- Details the rights and responsibilities of transmission owners and transmission customers as well as the procedures they must follow and the fees transmission customers must pay to access the transmission system\n- Sets out the transmission system planning process for the region\n\n## Resources and Tools\n\n- ISO Planning Procedure No. 5-6(PP 5-6), Scope of Study for System Impact Studies under the Generation Interconnection Procedures—describes the scope of interconnection studies conducted pursuant to Schedule 22, Schedule 23, and Schedule 25.\n- For additional resources for the Interconnection Process see Interconnection Service.\n- “Interconnection Process” training is available on the Training Materials webpage.\n- See the IRTT public queue. (Log in not necessary.) I F I C A T I O N\n\nProposed Compliance Overview\n\n## Order 2023 - Improvements to Generator Interconnection Procedures and Agreements\n\n##### Project Title: Order No. 2023 Compliance Proposed Effective Date: March 2024\n\n• ISO New England is preparing its compliance with Federal Energy Regulatory Commission (“FERC”) Order No. 2023 \n• This presentation provides: – A summary description of the Order – An overview of ISO’s proposed compliance – Anticipated stakeholder schedule \n\n### SUMMARY DESCRIPTION OF ORDER 2023\n\n### Order No. 2023 Development\n\n• On July 28, 2023, FERC issued Order No. 2023, an approximately 1500-page unanimous Final Rule adopting significant reforms to the standard Large Generator Interconnection Procedures (“LGIP”) and Small Generator Interconnection Procedure (“SGIP”) \n• The reforms build on the standardized procedures that FERC established in Order Nos. 2003, 2006 and 845 to address interconnection queue backlogs, improve certainty, and prevent undue discrimination for new technologies \n• Order No. 2023 adopts most of the reforms proposed in the July 5, 2022 Notice of Proposed Rulemaking (“NOPR”) \n\n### The Need for Reform\n\n• In Order No. 2023, FERC affirmed their preliminary findings that the existing standardized interconnection procedures and agreements are insufficient to ensure interconnection to the transmission system in a reliable, efficient, transparent, and timely manner – The growth of new resources seeking to interconnect and the differing characteristics of those resources have created new challenges for the process \n• These new challenges contribute to queue backlogs and uncertainty regarding the cost and timing of interconnecting, increasing costs for consumers, which, in turn, can create reliability issues, as needed new generating facilities are unable to come online in an efficient and timely manner \n\n### The Need for Reform, continued\n\n• FERC concludes that, absent reforms, the current process will continue to cause queue backlogs, longer development timelines, and increased uncertainty regarding the cost and timing of interconnecting to the transmission system – Accordingly, pursuant to Federal Power Act Section 206, FERC adopted significant, non-regionally tailored, reforms in the standard LGIP and SGIP \n\n### Order No. 2023 Compliance\n\n• FERC will evaluate compliance filings in light of the independent entity variations standard – Permits ISO-NE to seek to preserve certain variations implemented over the years to improve queue management – However, the order requires significant changes to the manner in which ISO-NE administers the interconnection processes, as well as how interconnection customers are expected to participate in those processes \n• Order No. 2023 was published in the Federal Register on September 6, 2023, setting a compliance deadline of December 5, 2023 \n\n##### Primary Elements of the Order Transition to First-Ready First-Served Cluster Process\n\n• Requires that transmission providers make several changes to transition to a first-ready, first-served cluster study process, rather than the current serial first-come, first-served study process – Cluster study process – Increased financial commitments for interconnection customers to enter and remain in the process – New mechanisms for interconnection customers to share interconnection study and upgrade costs – Enhanced site control requirements – Requirements for interconnection customers to select a definitive point of interconnection – Commercial readiness deposit requirements – Withdrawal penalties \n\n##### Primary Elements of the Order, continued Increase the Speed of Interconnection Processing\n\n• Eliminates the reasonable efforts standard for completing interconnection studies, and establishes firm study deadlines along with financial penalties on transmission providers that fail to meet them \n• Adopts a uniform approach to Affected System coordination \n\n### Primary Elements of the Order, continued Incorporate Technological Advancements\n\n• Allow co-location of multiple generating facilities behind a single point of interconnection \n• Allow interconnection customers to access surplus interconnection once the original interconnection customer has an executed LGIA (or an unexecuted filed LGIA) \n• Allow interconnection customer-provided operating assumptions in interconnection studies that change the proposed charging behavior of an electric storage resource \n• Incorporate specific alternative transmission technologies for evaluation during the interconnection study process \n• Incorporate modeling and ride-through requirements for non synchronous generating facilities \n\nREVIEW OF ORDER       "text": "FEBRUARY 15, 2024 | NEPOOL TRANSMISSION COMMITTEE | DOUBLETREE HOTEL, MILFORD, M A\n\nAl McBride D I R E C T O R | T R A N S M I S S I O N S E R V I C E S & R E S O U R C E Q U A L I F I C A T I O N\n\n#### Summary of Tariff Redline Updates since the January TC Meeting\n\n## Order No. 2023 – Improvements to Generator Interconnection Procedures and Agreements\n\nISO-NE PUBLIC 2\n\n### Presentation Overview\n\n• ISO has posted an updated draft of all Tariff redlines associated with Order No. 2023 compliance \n• This presentation summarizes the major updates to the Tariff redlines since the January TC meeting \n• Focus on Large Generator Interconnection Procedures (LGIP) in Schedule 22 of the Open Access Transmission Tariff (OATT) – The Small Generator Interconnection Procedures (SGIP) in Schedule 23 of the OATT have been overhauled to mirror the LGIP – The Elective Transmission Upgrade Interconnection Procedures (ETUIP) in Schedule 25 of the OATT also closely match the LGIP \n\nISO-NE PUBLIC ISO-NE INTERNAL USE\n\n### UPDATES TO THE TARIFF REDLINES SINCE THE\n\n### JANUARY TC MEETING\n\n##### Tariff Section Tariff Change Reason for Change\n\nLGIP Section 3.1\n\nCommercial Readiness Deposits that must be submitted to the System Operator under this LGIP must be (a) delivered to the System Operator’s bank account by electronic transfer, or (b) through the provision and maintenance of an irrevocable lLetter of cCredit in a form and from a financial institution acceptable to System Operator and included on the List of Eligible Letter of Credit Issuers, as described on the System Operator’s public website, or (c) a combination thereof. Each letter of credit must specify the Interconnection Request to which it corresponds. All costs associated with obtaining a letter of credit shall be borne by the Interconnection Customer. In the event that System Operator identifies an administrative deficiency with a submitted letter of credit, Interconnection Customer shall have five (5) Business Days to cure the deficiency..\n\nIn response to stakeholder suggestion, a cure period has been included for certain Letter of Credit (LOC) issues. Note that an LOC, in the correct amount, must have been submitted by the relevant deadline\n\n##### Tariff Section Tariff Change Reason for Change\n\nLGIP Section 3.1\n\nIf the System Operator removes the financial institution from the list, Interconnection Customer shall have five (5) Business Days from the date on which System Operator provides notice of such removal to replace the letter of credit with a letter of credit from a financial institution on the list. The System Operator may extend this cure period in its sole discretion. Failure to cure a deficiency within the periods prescribed in this Section 3.1 shall result in the withdrawal of the Interconnection Request pursuant to Section 3.7 of the LGIP without further opportunity to cure.\n\nAdded cure period for certain LOC issues. Note that an LOC, in the correct amount, must have been submitted by the relevant deadline\n\n##### Tariff Section Tariff Change Reason for Change\n\nLGIP Definitions\n\nTransitional Capacity Network Resource Group Study (“Transitional CNR Group Study”) shall mean the study performed by the System Operator under Section III.13.1.1.2.3A of the Tariff and Section 5.1.1.3 of this LGIP.\n\nDefinition for the Transitional Capacity Network Resource Group Study\n\n##### Tariff Section Tariff Change Reason for Change\n\nLGIP Section 3.2.1.3\n\nAny Interconnection Customer with a valid Interconnection Request for CNR Interconnection Service that has a completed Interconnection System Impact Study as of May 1, 2024, but that has not received a Capacity Supply Obligation through eighteenth Forward Capacity Auction or an earlier auction may: 1) seek to complete the process for obtaining CNR Interconnection Service through the process described in Section III.13.1.1.2A of the Tariff or 2) seek to complete the process for obtaining CNR Interconnection Service through the Transitional Cluster Study.       "text": "Interconnection Process Guide\n\n/isoexpress/webservices/-/webservice/getSignInInfo\n\n/isoexpress/webservices/-/webservice/authenticate\n\n# Interconnection Process Guide\n\n### In this section\n\n- Interconnection Process Guide\n- Is the ISO New England Interconnection Process Right for You?\n- Register for an IRTT Account\n- Submit an Interconnection Request During Cluster Request Window\n- Register Your Asset\n\n### Related Links\n\n- Glossary of Interconnection-Related Acronyms\n- Interconnection-Related Governing Documents\n- Contact IRTT Support\n\nISO New England is providing this web guide to assist you in understanding the interconnection process for small and large generating facilities and elective transmission upgrades (ETUs) to interconnect to the administered transmission system. However, you shouldn’t rely solely on this web guide. Consult the ISO New England Inc. Transmission, Markets, and Services Tariff(ISO Tariff), Section II, known as the ISO’s Open Access Transmission Tariff(OATT), and relevant planning procedures and operating procedures to ensure that you are complying with all requirements. In case of any discrepancy with this web guide, the ISO Tariff and applicable operating procedures and planning procedures govern.\n\n## Overview of the Interconnection Process\n\nAs a Regional Transmission Organization (RTO), the ISO coordinates how transmission lines, power plants, and other resources connect to and operate on New England’s administered transmission system to ensure reliability and facilitate wholesale electricity market participation. The ISO does not administer the interconnection process for projects subject to state interconnection processes. Thus, after you determine that the ISO interconnection process applies to your project, you’ll need to submit an interconnection request to the ISO through the Interconnection Request Tracking Tool(IRTT). Existing customers can register for an account using IRTT, while new customers must first or simultaneously obtain an ISO customer ID and access to critical energy infrastructure information.\n\nAs required by the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission’s Order No. 2023, it is only possible to submit interconnection requests during a cluster request window. The next window to submit a complete interconnection request package for the next Cluster Study is expected to be open from October 5, 2026 to November 19, 2026. After the ISO validates your interconnection request and the cluster request window closes, the customer engagement window opens and a cluster study scoping meeting is held. A cluster study scoping meeting will address pertinent information about your project and the interconnection studies the ISO will perform to assess your project’s impact on the power system and the facilities and upgrades potentially needed to interconnect your project per all requirements and standards.\n\nAfter the ISO has completed the necessary study work, you’ll work with the ISO and the transmission owner on an Interconnection Agreement (IA), a formal document detailing the terms and conditions for interconnection service. You must enter into an IA before you can construct or operate a generator or an elective transmission upgrade. Final steps of the interconnection process are to submit final data and register your asset with the ISO.\n\n## Governing Documents\n\nSeveral schedules of the ISO’s Open Access Transmission Tariff contain the rules detailing the interconnection process:\n\nSection II, ISO New England Open Access Transmission Tariff(OATT)\n\n- Helps ensure that all transmission owners and transmission customers have open access to transmission service in New England\n- Details the rights and responsibilities of transmission owners and transmission customers as well as the procedures they must follow and the fees transmission customers must pay to access the transmission system\n- Sets out the transmission system planning process for the region\n\n## Resources and Tools\n\n- ISO Planning Procedure No. 5-6(PP 5-6), Scope of Study for System Impact Studies under the Generation Interconnection Procedures—describes the scope of interconnection studies conducted pursuant to Schedule 22, Schedule 23, and Schedule 25.\n- For additional resources for the Interconnection Process see Interconnection Service.\n- “Interconnection Process” training is available on the Training Materials webpage.\n- See the IRTT public queue. (Log in not necessary.) Effective Date: March 2024\n\n• ISO New England is preparing its compliance with Federal Energy Regulatory Commission (“FERC”) Order No. 2023 \n• This presentation provides: – A summary description of the Order – An overview of ISO’s proposed compliance – Anticipated stakeholder schedule \n\n### SUMMARY DESCRIPTION OF ORDER 2023\n\n### Order No. 2023 Development\n\n• On July 28, 2023, FERC issued Order No. 2023, an approximately 1500-page unanimous Final Rule adopting significant reforms to the standard Large Generator Interconnection Procedures (“LGIP”) and Small Generator Interconnection Procedure (“SGIP”) \n• The reforms build on the standardized procedures that FERC established in Order Nos. 2003, 2006 and 845 to address interconnection queue backlogs, improve certainty, and prevent undue discrimination for new technologies \n• Order No. 2023 adopts most of the reforms proposed in the July 5, 2022 Notice of Proposed Rulemaking (“NOPR”) \n\n### The Need for Reform\n\n• In Order No. 2023, FERC affirmed their preliminary findings that the existing standardized interconnection procedures and agreements are insufficient to ensure interconnection to the transmission system in a reliable, efficient, transparent, and timely manner – The growth of new resources seeking to interconnect and the differing characteristics of those resources have created new challenges for the process \n• These new challenges contribute to queue backlogs and uncertainty regarding the cost and timing of interconnecting, increasing costs for consumers, which, in turn, can create reliability issues, as needed new generating facilities are unable to come online in an efficient and timely manner \n\n### The Need for Reform, continued\n\n• FERC concludes that, absent reforms, the current process will continue to cause queue backlogs, longer development timelines, and increased uncertainty regarding the cost and timing of interconnecting to the transmission system – Accordingly, pursuant to Federal Power Act Section 206, FERC adopted significant, non-regionally tailored, reforms in the standard LGIP and SGIP \n\n### Order No. 2023 Compliance\n\n• FERC will evaluate compliance filings in light of the independent entity variations standard – Permits ISO-NE to seek to preserve certain variations implemented over the years to improve queue management – However, the order requires significant changes to the manner in which ISO-NE administers the interconnection processes, as well as how interconnection customers are expected to participate in those processes \n• Order No. 2023 was published in the Federal Register on September 6, 2023, setting a compliance deadline of December 5, 2023 \n\n##### Primary Elements of the Order Transition to First-Ready First-Served Cluster Process\n\n• Requires that transmission providers make several changes to transition to a first-ready, first-served cluster study process, rather than the current serial first-come, share interconnection study and upgrade costs – Enhanced site control requirements – Requirements for interconnection customers to select a definitive point of interconnection – Commercial readiness deposit requirements – Withdrawal penalties \n\n##### Primary Elements of the Order, continued Increase the Speed of Interconnection Processing\n\n• Eliminates the reasonable efforts standard for completing interconnection studies, and establishes firm study deadlines along with financial penalties on transmission providers that fail to meet them \n• Adopts a uniform approach to Affected System coordination \n\n### Primary Elements of the Order, continued Incorporate Technological Advancements\n\n• Allow co-location of multiple generating facilities behind a single point of interconnection \n• Allow interconnection customers to access surplus interconnection once the original interconnection customer has an executed LGIA (or an unexecuted filed LGIA) \n• Allow interconnection customer-provided operating assumptions in interconnection studies that change the proposed charging behavior of an electric storage resource \n• Incorporate specific alternative transmission technologies for evaluation during the interconnection study process \n• Incorporate modeling and ride-through requirements for non synchronous generating facilities \n\nREVIEW OF ORDER       "text": "Order No. 2023 Key Project\n\n/isoexpress/webservices/-/webservice/getSignInInfo\n\n/isoexpress/webservices/-/webservice/authenticate\n\n# Order No. 2023 Key Project\n\n### In this section\n\n- Key Projects\n- Asset Condition Reviewer Key Project\n- Capacity Auction Reforms Key Project\n- Operational Impacts of Extreme Weather Events Key Project\n- Order No. 1920 Key Project\n- Order No. 2222 Key Project\n- Order No. 2023 Key Project\n- Implemented Key Projects\n\n### Related Links\n\n- Annual Work Plan\n- Participant Readiness Project Outlook\n- Training\n- Wholesale Markets Project Plan\n\nAccess documents and resources related to Order No. 2023: Improvements to Generator Interconnection Procedures and Agreements Key Project.\n\n### Project Overview\n\nOn July 28, 2023, the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission (FERC) issued Order No. 2023 (RM22-14-000) mandating significant reforms to generator interconnection procedures and agreements to ensure that interconnection customers can interconnect to the transmission system in a reliable, efficient, transparent, timely, and fair manner. The reforms build on the standardized procedures that FERC established in Order Nos. 2003, 2006, and 845, and are intended to address transmission interconnection queue backlogs, improve certainty, and prevent undue discrimination for new technologies as those resources proliferate. On March 21, 2024, FERC issued Order No. 2023-A with some incremental reforms to the Order.\n\nComplying with the Order requires significant changes to the manner in which the ISO administers the transmission interconnection processes and how interconnection customers are expected to participate in those processes.\n\nPrimary elements of the Order include:\n\n- Implementing a first-ready, first-served cluster study process, eliminating New England’s current serial first-come, first-served study process\n- Speeding up interconnection queue processing through improved processes, deadlines, and penalties\n- Incorporating technological advancements into the interconnection process, including modeling and performance standards for inverter-based resources\n\n### Learning Resources\n\nBelow are important Q&A and training materials for participation in the interconnection process for the upcoming Order No. 2023 Transitional Cluster Study:\n\n- Read questions and answers on the Order Nos. 2023/2023-A transition\n- Explore Order No. 2023 and Transitional Cluster Study training resources\n- Access Knowledge Articles in Ask ISO on the 2025 Transitional Interconnection Process\n- View the ISO Public Interconnection Queue\n- Read the Interconnection Process Guide on the ISO website\n\n### Commercial Readiness Deposit (CRD) Requirements\n\nBelow are the CRD instructions and other forms required for participation in the interconnection process for the upcoming Order No. 2023 Transitional Cluster Study:\n\n- CRD Instructions, FAQs, and ISO-NE Approved I F I C A T I O N\n\nProposed Compliance Overview\n\n## Order 2023 - Improvements to Generator Interconnection Procedures and Agreements\n\n##### Project Title: Order No. 2023 Compliance Proposed Effective Date: March 2024\n\n• ISO New England is preparing its compliance with Federal Energy Regulatory Commission (“FERC”) Order No. 2023 \n• This presentation provides: – A summary description of the Order – An overview of ISO’s proposed compliance – Anticipated stakeholder schedule \n\n### SUMMARY DESCRIPTION OF ORDER 2023\n\n### Order No. 2023 Development\n\n• On July 28, 2023, FERC issued Order No. 2023, an approximately 1500-page unanimous Final Rule adopting significant reforms to the standard Large Generator Interconnection Procedures (“LGIP”) and Small Generator Interconnection Procedure (“SGIP”) \n• The reforms build on the standardized procedures that FERC established in Order Nos. 2003, 2006 and 845 to address interconnection queue backlogs, improve certainty, and prevent undue discrimination for new technologies \n• Order No. 2023 adopts most of the reforms proposed in the July 5, 2022 Notice of Proposed Rulemaking (“NOPR”) \n\n### The Need for Reform\n\n• In Order No. 2023, FERC affirmed their preliminary findings that the existing standardized interconnection procedures and agreements are insufficient to ensure interconnection to the transmission system in a reliable, efficient, transparent, and timely manner – The growth of new resources seeking to interconnect and the differing characteristics of those resources have created new challenges for the process \n• These new challenges contribute to queue backlogs and uncertainty regarding the cost and timing of interconnecting, increasing costs for consumers, which, in turn, can create reliability issues, as needed new generating facilities are unable to come online in an efficient and timely manner \n\n### The Need for Reform, continued\n\n• FERC concludes that, absent reforms, the current process will continue to cause queue backlogs, longer development timelines, and increased uncertainty regarding the cost and timing of interconnecting to the transmission system – Accordingly, pursuant to Federal Power Act Section 206, FERC adopted significant, non-regionally tailored, reforms in the standard LGIP and SGIP \n\n### Order No. 2023 Compliance\n\n• FERC will evaluate compliance filings in light of the independent entity variations standard – Permits ISO-NE to seek to preserve certain variations implemented over the years to improve queue management – However, the order requires significant changes to the manner in which ISO-NE administers the interconnection processes, as well as how interconnection customers are expected to participate in those processes \n• Order No. 2023 was published in the Federal Register on September 6, 2023, setting a compliance deadline of December 5, 2023 \n\n##### Primary Elements of the Order Transition to First-Ready First-Served Cluster Process\n\n• Requires that transmission providers make several changes to transition to a first-ready, first-served cluster study process, rather than the current serial first-come, share interconnection study and upgrade costs – Enhanced site control requirements – Requirements for interconnection customers to select a definitive point of interconnection – Commercial readiness deposit requirements – Withdrawal penalties \n\n##### Primary Elements of the Order, continued Increase the Speed of Interconnection Processing\n\n• Eliminates the reasonable efforts standard for completing interconnection studies, and establishes firm study deadlines along with financial penalties on transmission providers that fail to meet them \n• Adopts a uniform approach to Affected System coordination \n\n### Primary Elements of the Order, continued Incorporate Technological Advancements\n\n• Allow co-location of multiple generating facilities behind a single point of interconnection \n• Allow interconnection customers to access surplus interconnection once the original interconnection customer has an executed LGIA (or an unexecuted filed LGIA) \n• Allow interconnection customer-provided operating assumptions in interconnection studies that change the proposed charging behavior of an electric storage resource \n• Incorporate specific alternative transmission technologies for evaluation during the interconnection study process \n• Incorporate modeling and ride-through requirements for non synchronous generating facilities \n\nREVIEW OF ORDER The 30 CD is a no later than date.\n\nQ3: How does ISO-NE define a completed Facilities Study?\n\nResponse: ISO-NE defines a completed FAC as the acceptance of a FAC report by the IC during the comment period, which is within 30 CD of receipt of the FAC report. The 30 CD is a no later than date.\n\nQ4: If a Facilities Study is in progress as of the Eligibility Date, will the customer have the option to waive its completion and go directly into LGIA negotiations? # Requirements and Fees for a Valid Large Generator Interconnection Application in ISO New England

Based on the **ISO-NE Tariff Schedule 22 – Large Generator Interconnection Procedures (LGIP)** (effective May 3, 2025, per FERC Docket ER25-2149-000), the **ISO-NE Interconnection Process Guide** (web guide), the **Appendix 1 Interconnection Request** form, the **ISO-NE Interconnection Technical Notice on Site Control** (June 2, 2025), and associated FERC Order No. 2023 compliance materials, the following is a comprehensive summary of what is required to submit a valid interconnection application for a Large Generating Facility (>20 MW) in ISO New England.

---

## 1. Pre-Application Prerequisites

Before submitting an interconnection request (IR), the Interconnection Customer must complete three registration steps:

| Requirement | Description | Timeline |
|---|---|---|
| **ISO Customer ID** | Obtain an ISO New England Customer ID by submitting a "Request Generation Interconnect Customer ID" form to memcoord@iso-ne.com. The customer name must match across all ISO registrations. | 5–10 business days |
| **CEII Access** | Request and obtain Interconnection Request (IR) Critical Energy Infrastructure Information (CEII) clearance through the ISO's Request CEII Access page. | 5–10 business days |
| **IRTT Account** | Register for an account in the Interconnection Request Tracking Tool (IRTT). Existing customers can register directly; new customers must first obtain a Customer ID. | ~10 business days |

> **Source:** ISO-NE Interconnection Process Guide – "Register for an IRTT Account" and "Submit an Interconnection Request During Cluster Request Window" pages ([iso-ne.com](https://www.iso-ne.com/participate/applications-status-changes/interconnection-process-guide/submit-an-interconnection-request)); ISO-NE Interconnection Process Training, February 16, 2023 ([PDF](https://www.iso-ne.com/static-assets/documents/2023/02/20230216-interconnection-process-presentation.pdf)).

---

## 2. Timing: Cluster Request Window

Pursuant to FERC Order No. 2023, ISO-NE has transitioned from a serial first-come, first-served process to a **first-ready, first-served cluster study process**. Interconnection requests can **only** be submitted during a **45-calendar-day Cluster Request Window**.

- The next Cluster Request Window is expected to be open from **October 5, 2026 to November 19, 2026**.
- All IRs submitted and validated within a single Cluster Request Window are considered **equally queued** (i.e., they receive the same queue position relative to each other).

> **Source:** ISO-NE Interconnection Process Guide ([iso-ne.com](https://www.iso-ne.com/participate/applications-status-changes/interconnection-process-guide)); Schedule 22, Section 4.1.1; ISO-NE Order No. 2023 Compliance Overview, September 27, 2023 ([PDF](https://www.iso-ne.com/static-assets/documents/100003/a04a_2023_09_27_tc_order2023_compliance_overview.pdf)).

---

## 3. Completed Interconnection Request Form (Appendix 1 to Schedule 22)

The IR must be submitted using the **Appendix 1 Interconnection Request** form, which requires the following information:

### A. Project Information
- **Proposed Project Name**
- **Purpose of the request** (check one):
  - A proposed new Large Generating Facility
  - An increase in generating capacity or a modification that has the potential to be a Material Modification of an existing Generating Facility
  - Commencement of participation in the wholesale markets by an existing Generating Facility
  - A change from Network Resource Interconnection Service to Capacity Network Resource Interconnection Service

### B. Type of Interconnection Service Requested (check one)
- **Network Resource (NR) Interconnection Service** — energy capability only; enables participation in all energy markets, except the capacity market.
- **Capacity Network Resource (CNR) Interconnection Service** — energy and capacity capability; enables participation in both energy and capacity markets.
- Option to request downgrade to NR Service if violations are identified in the thermal analysis associated with CNR testing.

### C. Technical/Project Details
- **Address or location of the facility** (including Town/City, County, and State)
- **Requested Point of Interconnection (POI)**
- **Type of Generating Facility** to be constructed
- **Electric storage capacity** information (if applicable): whether the facility includes electric storage, whether it charges from the Administered Transmission System, aggregate charging capability at the POI, reactive capability at the high side of the main transformer, and primary frequency response operating range
- **Generating Facility Fuel Type**
- **Generating Facility Capacity (MW)** at four temperature bands:
  - At or above 90°F, 50°F, 20°F, and 0°F
  - For each: Maximum Gross MW Electrical Output, Maximum Net MW Electrical Output, and Net MW Capability at the POI
- **Requested Interconnection Service levels (in MW)**: CNR Capability (Summer/Winter), NR Capability (Summer/Winter)
- **Equipment configuration description**, including number of units, GSUs, and any proposed control technologies to restrict output
- **Requested Commercial Operation Date**
- **Requested Initial Synchronization Date**
- **Requested In-Service Date**

### D. Customer Information
- Company Name, ISO Customer ID#, address, company representative name/title/phone/email
- Authorized signature, printed name, title, and date

> **Source:** Appendix 1 to Schedule 22 – Interconnection Request form ([PDF](https://www.iso-ne.com/static-assets/documents/100030/oatt-schedule-22-appendix-1-large-generator-lgip-interconnection-request-with-attachments.pdf)); ISO-NE Interconnection Process Guide – "Submit an Interconnection Request" page.

---

## 4. Financial Requirements: Deposits and Fees

A valid IR must be accompanied by **multiple financial commitments** at the time of submission during the Cluster Request Window. The following table summarizes all required deposits:

| Deposit/Fee | Amount | Refundable? | Applied Toward |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Initial Deposit (Application Fee)** | **$50,000** | Potentially non-refundable (becomes non-refundable 10 business days after the scoping meeting) | Costs incurred by the ISO associated with the IR, development of study agreements and their attachments, and development of the LGIA |
| **Study Deposit** (new NR or CNR request) | **$250,000** | Refundable (applied to study costs; unspent portions refunded) | Cost of the Cluster Study |
| **Study Deposit** (request to change from existing NR to CNR) | **$100,000** | Refundable | Cost of the Cluster Study |
| **Commercial Readiness Deposit (CRD)** | Per FERC Order No. 2023 requirements (ISO-NE adopted the Order's CRD framework; the standard amount under Order No. 2023 is based on generating facility size, with ISO-NE's implementation detailed in the CRD Instructions on the Order No. 2023 Key Project page) | May be provided as cash (electronic transfer) or irrevocable Letter of Credit from an approved financial institution, or a combination | Demonstrates commercial readiness to proceed; subject to forfeiture upon withdrawal |
| **Site Control Deposit** (in lieu of Site Control, NR only, if Regulatory Limitation claimed) | **$10,000/MW**, subject to a **minimum of $500,000** and a **maximum of $2,000,000** | Refundable within the cure period per Section 3.4.3 of the LGIP | Held until Site Control is demonstrated; must demonstrate full Site Control within 180 calendar days of executing an Interconnection Agreement |

### Additional Study-Stage Deposits (Later in the Process)

| Deposit | Amount | Trigger |
|---|---|---|
| **Cluster Restudy Deposit** (if required) | Additional deposit equal to **5% of the network upgrade cost** assigned from the Cluster Study | If a Cluster Restudy is needed |
| **Interconnection Facilities Study Deposit** | The **greater of 25% of the estimated cost of the Facilities Study or $250,000** | When entering the Facilities Study stage |
| **Actual Study Costs** | Interconnection Customer pays actual costs of all studies; invoiced monthly | Throughout the study process |

> **Sources:**
> - Schedule 22, Section 3.4.1.1 — initial deposit and study deposit amounts ([PDF](https://www.iso-ne.com/static-assets/documents/regulatory/tariff/sect_2/sch22/sch_22_lgip.pdf))
> - Appendix 1 to Schedule 22 — Site Control deposit ($10,000/MW, min $500K, max $2M) ([PDF](https://www.iso-ne.com/static-assets/documents/100030/oatt-schedule-22-appendix-1-large-generator-lgip-interconnection-request-with-attachments.pdf))
> - Appendix 3 to Schedule 22 — Facilities Study deposit (greater of 25% or $250,000) ([PDF](https://www.iso-ne.com/static-assets/documents/100030/oatt-schedule-22-appendix-3-large-generator-lgip-interconnection-facilities-study-agreement.pdf))
> - ISO-NE Order No. 2023 Compliance Overview, September 27, 2023 — Cluster Restudy deposit (5% of network upgrade cost) and CRD framework ([PDF](https://www.iso-ne.com/static-assets/documents/100003/a04a_2023_09_27_tc_order2023_compliance_overview.pdf))
> - ISO-NE Order No. 2023 Compliance Presentation, January 23, 2024 — Section 3.4.1.1 deposit language ([PDF](https://www.iso-ne.com/static-assets/documents/100007/a03.1b_2024_01_23_tc_order2023_compliance_presentation.pdf))
> - Eversource summary of ISO-NE deposits (as of April 1, 2023) ([eversource.com](https://www.eversource.com/business/about/doing-business-with-us/transmission-interconnections/transmission-interconnection-process))
> - ISO-NE Order No. 2023 Key Project page — CRD Instructions, FAQs, and approved templates ([iso-ne.com](https://iso-ne.com/committees/key-projects/order-no-2023-key-project))
> - ISO-NE Interconnection Technical Notice on Site Control, June 2, 2025 ([PDF](https://www.iso-ne.com/static-assets/documents/100024/notice_re_regulatory_limitations_and_site_control_6_2_25.pdf))

---

## 5. Site Control Requirements

Site Control is a critical validity requirement. The rules differ by service type:

### For CNR Interconnection Service (Capacity):
- **100% exclusive Site Control** in the Interconnection Customer's name is **required without exception**.

### For NR Interconnection Service (Energy):
- **100% exclusive Site Control** in the Interconnection Customer's name is required, **OR**
- In lieu of Site Control, if a **Regulatory Limitation** prevents obtaining Site Control prior to submission, the customer may submit:
  1. A **$10,000/MW cash deposit** (minimum $500,000; maximum $2,000,000), refundable within the cure period per Section 3.4.3;
  2. A **signed affidavit** from an officer of the company indicating that Site Control is unobtainable due to Regulatory Limitations; and
  3. **Documentation** sufficiently describing and explaining the source and effects of such Regulatory Limitations, including conditions that must be met and the anticipated time to satisfy them.

### For Modifications to Existing Facilities:
- Site Control documentation is not required if the proposed modification is to the customer's existing Large Generating Facility and does not require additional real property (customer certifies it has Site Control).

### Acceptable Forms of Site Control Documentation:
- (a) Fee simple ownership or easement;
- (b) Valid written leasehold or other contractual interest;
- (c) Valid written option to purchase or leasehold interest;
- (d) Duly executed written contract to purchase, acquire an easement, license, or leasehold; or
- (e) Filed applications for required permits to site on federal or state property.

### Timing:
- Site Control documents must be submitted **as part of the IR through IRTT during the Cluster Request Window**.
- If a Regulatory Limitation is accepted, the customer must demonstrate **full Site Control within 180 calendar days** of executing an Interconnection Agreement.

> **Source:** ISO-NE Interconnection Technical Notice on Site Control, June 2, 2025 ([PDF](https://www.iso-ne.com/static-assets/documents/100024/notice_re_regulatory_limitations_and_site_control_6_2_25.pdf)); Appendix 1 to Schedule 22; Schedule 22, Section 3.4.2.

---

## 6. Technical Data Requirements

In addition to the Appendix 1 form, the Interconnection Customer must submit **technical data** sufficient for the ISO to perform the Cluster Study. The ISO provides guidance through the **Interconnection Request Technical Data Submittal Guidance** document. Key technical data includes:

- **Site electrical one-line diagram** reflecting the Generating Facility's configuration
- **Generator/converter specifications** (e.g., manufacturer model sheets, P-Q capability curves, ride-through characteristics for inverter-based resources)
- **Short circuit and dynamic modeling data** (e.g., manufacturer-provided models in PSS/E, PSCAD, or equivalent formats)
- **Reactive power capability** data
- **Primary frequency response** parameters (especially for electric storage resources)
- **Point of Interconnection** details and any proposed interconnection facilities

The ISO references **Planning Procedure No. 5-6 (PP 5-6)** for the scope and assumptions of interconnection studies, and **Operating Procedure 14 (OP-14)** for technical requirements for generators.

> **Source:** ISO-NE Interconnection Process Guide – "Submit an Interconnection Request" page; ISO-NE Interconnection Process Training, February 16, 2023 ([PDF](https://www.iso-ne.com/static-assets/documents/2023/02/20230216-interconnection-process-presentation.pdf)).

---

## 7. Validation Process

Once the IR is submitted through IRTT during the Cluster Request Window:

1. **Within 5 business days** of receipt, the ISO will notify the Interconnection Customer whether the IR is **valid** or identify any **deficiencies** (including deficiencies related to Site Control, deposits, or technical data).
2. If deficiencies are identified, the customer has a **cure period** (generally 10 business days) to cure the deficiencies.
3. Once the IR is deemed valid, the ISO assigns a **Queue Position**. All valid IRs submitted within the same Cluster Request Window are considered **equally queued**.
4. Within **3 business days** after the close of the Cluster Request Window, the ISO submits copies of all valid IRs to the relevant Interconnecting Transmission Owner(s).

> **Source:** Schedule 22, Sections 3.4.4 and 4.1.1; ISO-NE Interconnection Technical Notice on Site Control; ISO-NE Order No. 2023 Compliance Presentation, January 23, 2024 ([PDF](https://www.iso-ne.com/static-assets/documents/100007/a03.1b_2024_01_23_tc_order2023_compliance_presentation.pdf)).

---

## 8. Summary Checklist for a Valid Large Generator Interconnection Request

| # | Requirement | Details |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | ISO Customer ID | Obtained prior to submission |
| 2 | CEII Access | IR CEII clearance obtained |
| 3 | IRTT Account | Active account in the Interconnection Request Tracking Tool |
| 4 | Submit during Cluster Request Window | 45-calendar-day window (next: Oct 5 – Nov 19, 2026) |
| 5 | Completed Appendix 1 Interconnection Request form | All project, technical, and customer information fields completed and signed |
| 6 | Initial Deposit | $50,000 (potentially non-refundable) |
| 7 | Study Deposit | $250,000 (new NR/CNR) or $100,000 (NR-to-CNR change) |
| 8 | Commercial Readiness Deposit (CRD) | Per Order No. 2023 requirements; cash or Letter of Credit |
| 9 | Site Control | 100% exclusive Site Control for CNR (mandatory); 100% for NR or $10,000/MW deposit (min $500K, max $2M) + affidavit + documentation if Regulatory Limitation claimed |
| 10 | Technical Data | Complete technical data package per PP 5-6 and the Interconnection Request Technical Data Submittal Guidance |
| 11 | Type of Service Election | NR (energy only) or CNR (energy + capacity) |
| 12 | Point of Interconnection | Definitive POI identified |

---

## Key Governing Documents

| Document | Description |
|---|---|
| **Schedule 22, LGIP** (effective 5/3/2025, ER25-2149-000) | Primary tariff governing large generator interconnection procedures |
| **Appendix 1 to Schedule 22** | Interconnection Request form |
| **Appendix 2 to Schedule 22** | Cluster Study Agreement
